skill.name: browser-extension-developer
skill.description: Use this skill when developing or maintaining browser extension code in the `browser/` directory, including Chrome/Firefox/Edge compatibility, content scripts, background scripts, or i18n updates.
skill.summary: Guide WXT browser extension development across Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and localization workflows.
skill.author: yamadashy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution and weak cryptography. Review found no executable code, cryptographic implementation, prompt injection, network calls, or data exfiltration in SKILL.md. The only retained concern is low-risk guidance to run normal npm development commands in the target project.

low_findings.title: Project npm Command Guidance
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: SKILL.md lists npm development, build, lint, and test commands. This is expected for a browser extension development skill, but npm scripts execute project-defined commands and should be run only in trusted repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit npm scripts in a documentation section. They are legitimate development commands, but they still invoke external project tooling.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis identified Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited content is Markdown frontmatter, a fenced structure example, inline command names, and an i18n path. No executable Ruby or shell code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows only Markdown formatting and user-facing command documentation. There is no code path that executes backtick contents.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithms at SKILL.md lines 3 and 31. Those lines describe browser extension scope and an i18n messages file path, with no cryptographic API or algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural language and a messages.json path. No MD5, SHA-1, cipher, hashing call, or crypto library appears in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Browser extension work often breaks across browsers, scripts, and locales. This skill gives focused guidance for WXT extension structure, commands, compatibility, and i18n updates.
actual_capabilities: Guides work on browser extension code under the browser directory.
Identifies the WXT framework as the extension foundation.
Covers Chrome, Firefox, and Edge compatibility considerations.
Points to background and content script entry points.
Lists development, build, lint, and test commands for extension work.
Documents the supported i18n locale set and required message keys.
limitations: It contains guidance only and does not include executable helper scripts.
It assumes the target repository already has the described browser directory structure.
It does not audit extension permissions or review store compliance.
It does not replace browser-specific manual testing.

use_cases.title: Add Cross-Browser Extension Features
use_cases.description: Use the skill to plan changes that affect Chrome, Firefox, and Edge extension behavior.

use_cases.title: Maintain WXT Build Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the command guidance to run development, build, lint, and test workflows for the extension.

use_cases.title: Update Extension Localization
use_cases.description: Use the locale guidance to add or review browser extension translation files.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Extension Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the browser extension structure and explain where I should change content scripts, background scripts, and locale files.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Localized Label
prompt_templates.prompt: Add or update the browser extension localization for a new user-facing label across the supported locale folders.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Browser Compatibility
prompt_templates.prompt: Investigate this browser extension issue and adjust the implementation for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge compatibility.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Release Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the browser extension changes, run the appropriate validation workflow, and summarize release risks for each supported browser.

output_examples.input: Review a content script change for cross-browser support.
output_examples.output: A compatibility review that identifies affected entry points, browser API differences, required polyfills, and validation commands.
A localization checklist covering the locale folder, required message keys, naming format, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Prepare the extension for build validation.
output_examples.output: A short build readiness summary with commands to run, expected artifacts, and issues that need manual browser testing.

best_practices: Run lint and tests before considering extension changes complete.
Check Chrome, Firefox, and Edge behavior when changing browser APIs.
Keep i18n message keys consistent across every supported locale.
anti_patterns: Changing only one browser target when the behavior affects shared extension code.
Adding visible extension text without updating locale message files.
Shipping build output without reviewing content script and background script behavior.

faq.question: What type of extension project does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets a WXT-based browser extension with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge support.

faq.question: Can it create a browser extension from scratch?
faq.answer: It is best for maintaining the described browser directory, not for scaffolding an unrelated extension.

faq.question: Does it cover localization?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the supported locales and the required appDescription and openWithRepomix message keys.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It lists project commands that a user or agent may choose to run during development.

faq.question: Which browser APIs does it mention?
faq.answer: It notes chrome.* APIs and warns that Firefox may need polyfills.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found no malicious code, with only low-risk npm command guidance retained as a caution.
